name: Decatastrophizing
tagline: Walk a fear all the way through to see it's survivable.
situations: 'What if it all goes wrong?' on a loop
A fear that feels too big to look at
summary: Decatastrophizing asks 'and then what?' until the feared catastrophe is followed all the way to the end. Instead of stopping at the terrifying image, you examine how likely it is, how bad it would really be, and how you'd cope. Most catastrophes shrink when you actually look at them.
howItWorks: Catastrophizing works by stopping at the worst image and treating it as both certain and unsurvivable. By continuing past that frozen frame — estimating real probability, distinguishing 'bad' from 'unbearable', and rehearsing how you would actually cope — you re-engage realistic appraisal. The fear loses its power not by being dismissed but by being fully examined.

steps.instruction: Name the catastrophe you're dreading, specifically.
Ask: realistically, how likely is this? What's the actual probability?
Ask 'and then what?' — follow it through to what you'd actually do next.
Identify how you would cope and who could help, then re-rate the fear.
steps.mantra: Even if it happened, I could handle it.

whenToUse: When you're spiraling into worst cases
Dread before an exam, flight, or hard event
When a fear feels too big to examine
To regain perspective on an unlikely outcome
whenNotToUse: When the feared outcome is likely and needs real planning
As a way to dismiss legitimate risks
In acute panic — calm the body first
tips: Estimate actual odds; we wildly overestimate catastrophe
Separate 'bad' from 'unbearable' — most fears are the former
Always end on a coping plan, not the fear
Write it out to keep the spiral from looping

evidence.summary: Decatastrophizing is a standard CBT/REBT technique; cognitive techniques for worry and anxiety have strong meta-analytic support.
citations: Ellis, A. (1962). Reason and Emotion in Psychotherapy. Lyle Stuart.
Hofmann, S.G. et al. (2012). The efficacy of CBT. Cognitive Therapy and Research.
